design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore non-linear levels as Scrooge McDuck, collect treasures and map pieces, defeat boss guardians, revisit levels for upgrades and hidden items
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Non-linear choice of level order creates exploration pacing tension; optional map piece collection gates hidden sixth stage; resource management via collected money for upgrades and consumables
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Adventurer-scholar collecting world treasures and solving environmental puzzles as the iconic Scrooge McDuck; treasure hunting with cane-based toolset
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately iterative sequel that refines predecessor's mechanics rather than innovating; emphasis on revisit-ability and optional content (map pieces, sixth stage) over mandatory progression; player choice in level order mirrors Mega Man design philosophy but applied to adventure-platformer

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with recovery items purchasable from store between levels
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-level checkpoint system; levels can be revisited after completion
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls level order; can grind money and purchase recovery items to trivialize difficulty; revisit mechanics allow level-specific practice

progression.upgrade_system: Cane upgrades from Gyro Gearloose grant access to new areas and hidden content; money currency collected from levels enables purchase of recovery items and extra lives
progression.unlock_mechanism: Five main stages freely selectable in any order; sixth optional stage unlocks only after collecting all five map pieces hidden in treasure chests; boss defeat required to progress stage completion state
replayability_hooks: Revisit completed levels to collect remaining money and items
Cane upgrades enable new paths in previously explored levels
Map piece collection incentivizes thorough exploration
Optional sixth stage with lost treasure of McDuck
Store purchases create resource management replay decisions

world_building_techniques: Level theming: Niagara Falls, Bermuda Triangle pirate ship, Mu, Egypt, Scotland establish geographic adventure narrative
Boss guardians as stage treasures: each area's unique treasure tied to thematic antagonist
Interstitial story: opening cutscene establishes map pursuit; ending narrative branches on treasure collection and money state
Character motivation: Flintheart Glomgold as recurring nemesis drives narrative tension
Family connection: Fergus McDuck's legacy treasure serves as narrative anchor

boss_design.philosophy: Stage guardians thematically matched to treasure location; designed as pattern-based encounters requiring cane jump mastery and environmental positioning
notable_bosses: D-1000 (shapeshifting robot final boss, Glomgold's true identity reveal)
Regional treasure guardians (5 main stages, names/specifics not documented)

faction_indicators: Scrooge McDuck (protagonist treasure hunter, family man)
Fergus McDuck (absent patriarch whose legacy drives plot)
danger_signals: Glomgold's betrayal via D-1000 robot assassination attempt
Ship sinking final hazard endangering protagonist and treasures
Webby kidnapping raises stakes beyond resource collection
Bad ending states: Glomgold steals lost treasure, player left with zero money
environmental_storytelling: Niagara Falls stage: natural wonder setting establishes exploration scope
Bermuda Triangle pirate ship: danger/mystery locale foreshadows betrayal
Mu stage: lost civilization theme connects to hidden treasure archaeology
Egypt stage: tomb raiding adventure archetype
Scotland stage: ancestral homeland ties to Fergus McDuck lineage
Treasure chests hidden in each stage: environmental clues to map piece locations

sources_used: speedrun: Speedrun.com leaderboard data showing 'Easy' difficulty classification consensus
tasvideos: Technical mechanics documentation on positioning, collision, interactive objects, RNG manipulation
tcrf: Game-Assist mechanical documentation, hidden level select, debugging functions
wikipedia: Plot structure, gameplay mechanics (revisit system, cane upgrades, map collection), character roster, bad/good ending conditions, reception context
